preamble: The shift from a voiceless velar stop [k] to a voiceless palatal stop [c] is a primary stage of palatalization, typically triggered by following front vowels (i, e) or the palatal glide [j]. This stage is often an intermediate step toward further affrication (to [tʃ] or [ts]), and is prominent in the Slavic First Palatalization and early Romance developments. In Hungarian, [c] (written 'ty') remains a distinct phoneme.

examples.note: Hungarian maintains a stable phonemic palatal stop /c/ (written 'ty') that historically developed via palatalization of velars or dentals.
Intermediate palatal stage before the shift to [tʃ] in Italian or [ts] > [s] in French.
The First Slavic Palatalization involved an intermediate [c] stage before [tʃ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
